doc.title: Coupled whole-tree optimality and xylem hydraulics explain dynamic biomass partitioning (Supporting Information)

model_card.name: THORP (soil hydrology module)
model_card.task: 1-D soil moisture / soil water potential dynamics coupled to root uptake and surface fluxes.

assumptions.text: 1-D vertical Richards equation (x positive upward) with Darcy flux q and source/sink term f.

evidence.snippet: Soil moisture is modeled by the 1-D vertical form of Richards equation (Richards 1931),

assumptions.text: Soil column discretized into N layers (z positive downward), Δz via geometric series; soil depth can be extended when groundwater table is deeper.

evidence.snippet: The soil column is discretized into multiple layers, indexed i, with thicknesses Δzi. The midpoint depth of each layer, zi, is used. The vertical spacings...

assumptions.text: Root-zone horizontal extent approximated by canopy width W when converting uptake to volumetric sink.

evidence.snippet: where ρ [kg m−3] is density of water, Δzi [m] is the thickness of soil layer i, and W [m] is the width of the canopy, an approximation of the horizontal extent...

assumptions.text: All precipitation infiltrates (q1=-P when P>0); when P=0, q1=E_soil.
Default lower boundary is free drainage (qN=-K_soil,N).

evidence.snippet: At the bottom of the soil column, we use a free drainage boundary condition, where 𝑞𝑁 = −𝐾𝑠𝑜𝑖𝑙,𝑁. (S.2.10)

assumptions.text: Hydraulics follow van Genuchten-type relations; K_sat and θ_s decline exponentially with depth.

evidence.snippet: We model hydraulic conductivity, Ksoil [m s−1], and hydraulic capacitance, Csoil [m3 m−3 MPa−1], using van Genuchten’s (1980) formulation:

assumptions.text: Soil evaporation uses a linearized Dalton approach and is zero when e_soil ≤ e_a.

evidence.snippet: 𝐸𝑠𝑜𝑖𝑙 = { 𝑚𝑤𝑔𝑎/(𝜌𝑃𝑎𝑡𝑚)(𝑒𝑠𝑜𝑖𝑙 − 𝑒𝑎), 𝑒𝑠𝑜𝑖𝑙 > 𝑒𝑎  0, 𝑒𝑠𝑜𝑖𝑙 ≤ 𝑒𝑎 (S.2.11)

assumptions.text: Implicit finite-difference discretization yields a tridiagonal system solved each time step.

evidence.snippet: In THORP, we solve Eq. S.2.1 by an implicit finite difference scheme. To do this, we expand Richards equation:

equations.latex: \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t}=C_{\mathrm{soil}}\frac{\partial \Psi_{\mathrm{soil}}}{\partial t}=-\frac{\partial q}{\partial x}+f=\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[K_{\mathrm{soil}}\!\left(\frac{1}{\varpi \rho g}\frac{\partial \Psi_{\mathrm{soil}}}{\partial x}+1\right)\right]+f

evidence.snippet: At the bottom of the soil column, we use a free drainage boundary condition, where 𝑞𝑁 = −𝐾𝑠𝑜𝑖𝑙,𝑁. (S.2.10)

equations.latex: a_i=\left[\frac{1}{\varpi\rho g}\left(\frac{K_i}{\Delta z_i}+K'_i\right)+\left(\frac{\partial K_i}{\partial \Psi_i}\right)^t\right]\frac{1}{\Delta z_{i-1\to i}}

variables.name: Soil water potential
variables.description: Primary state solved in the implicit soil hydrology scheme.

variables.name: Volumetric water content
variables.description: Derived from Ψ via retention curve.

variables.name: Soil water flux
variables.description: Darcy flux in the soil column.

variables.name: Sink/source term
variables.description: Root water uptake (negative) and hydraulic redistribution (positive).

variables.name: Effective saturation
variables.description: Effective saturation used in van Genuchten relations.

variables.name: Hydraulic conductivity
variables.description: Unsaturated conductivity computed from S_e and K_sat.

variables.name: Hydraulic capacitance
variables.description: Capacitance term in mixed-form Richards equation.

algorithm.overview: At each time step, compute sink terms and boundary fluxes, evaluate hydraulic properties, assemble a tridiagonal implicit system, solve for Ψ^{t+Δt}, then update θ and fluxes.

steps.description: Discretize soil column into N layers (z_i, Δz_i).

evidence.snippet: The soil column is discretized into multiple layers, indexed i, with thicknesses Δzi. The midpoint depth of each layer, zi, is used. The vertical spacings...

steps.description: Compute f_i from E_i (Eq. S.2.3) and set boundary fluxes q1,qN (Eqs. S.2.9–S.2.10).
Evaluate Se, θ, K, C (Eqs. S.2.4–S.2.8).
